author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: it's called a weak pullup usually and can anywhere from 30 to 200k ohms. It differs a lot even between controllers of the same type ( it's on the controller  ... not so much on the board .. ). This does away with switch current and for some types of switches that is bad. for sliding switches and gold contact switches it's fine. The minimum current can usually be found in the datasheet for switches and relay contacts.

author: @kenrowe167
text: Actually, Rob, your external resistor was connected to Gnd - so a pull down, with the switch connected to +V - so closing the switch turns the LED on, The internal pull up is conected to +V and your switch is connected to Gnd - so closing the switch grounds the input and the LED goes off.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: did you watch all of the video mate?

replies.author: @kenrowe167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes. I always do.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text: A very good point Ken, I suspect that a pull down resistor was used in the first demo sketch as it is more natural for people to think of the switch closing as turning the input "on".

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text: Any input generated by a switch will need to be debounced either by software or an RC filter. If you’re just driving a relay on an output pin you may not notice the output toggling up and down until it settles in the correct state.  But if you want to turn a function on or off via the switch you may end up in random on or off states. Switch contacts can bounce quite a lot and the Arduino is fast enough to see most of them as individual switch states, so you press the button once the Arduino see 10 or more.

author: @danielhayes4782
text: I realize that this is off topic from this video, but I wasn’t exactly sure which video it may ON topic for and I wasn’t sure how to contact you through another channel. But I have a question.... in theory, would it be possible to use a controlled flyback voltage as a way to make flash graphene? If you can predict a voltage spike, couldn’t you just put graphite directly in the path of that voltage spike?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not voltage mate - it's the amount of joules you put through it - so yes you could if your flyback can handle the amps - they tend no to be able to - but that doesn't mean they can't

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text: Input pull-up is quite handy, but as you said the original external pull-up was used to make it more noise-immune. If you're having issues with noise triggering the inputs, look into software debouncing code. It works by checking the value over a few milliseconds to make sure it's still pressed or unpressed. Obviously for limit switches you want to react immediately, so don't do that, but for trigger switches you want to be sure the trigger is properly pressed before reacting.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: @nil5221 Ah, I forgot about that, but either way as Rob said, a stronger pull-somewhere would be more noise-immune.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
The capacitive Sensing Library might be of interest.
Library does most of the coding work to replaces a mechanical switch by 1 resistor, a wire and optionally a conductive pad... as well as button, can then to make scroll wheels, sliders etc. all without needing to make cut outs in a plastic enclosure. I've printed out button layouts on transparancy sheet,, including nice neat txt labels, then glued the sheet outside of enclosure... sticky foil applied behind behind the appropriate points of the button layout, work to sense finger near button. likely your conductive ink would work too.
